content:
===BEGIN===
Inside every cell, DNA holds the master blueprint for life, but it stays safely locked away in the nucleus. To turn these blueprints into action, the cell uses a messenger called mRNA, which carries instructions from the DNA to the cell’s machinery. Think of mRNA as a courier delivering a copy of a specific recipe from a cookbook stored in a locked vault. Once the mRNA reaches the cell’s protein-making factories, called ribosomes, it guides them to assemble the right building blocks in the correct order to make proteins. These proteins carry out essential functions, from building structures to fighting infections. In short, mRNA acts as a vital link, translating the genetic information stored safely in DNA into real, functional proteins that keep the cell—and the entire body—running smoothly. This natural process is also the foundation for innovative mRNA-based vaccines and therapies.  
===END===
